exhibitions.title: Prosperity Nourished by Chinese Culture -- Basic Exhibition of Chinese Arts and Crafts
exhibitions.summary:
Chinese Arts and Crafts, nurtured by the essence of Chinese culture, embody the harmony between virtue and craftsmanship, reflecting the vicissitudes of centuries and integrating the aesthetic essence of skills. They demonstrate the continuity of ancient wisdom in contemporary times, echoing the spirit of cultural self-improvement of the nation.
Chinese Arts and Crafts boast a rich variety and diverse forms, resembling a myriad of treasures, each crafted with ingenuity. They uphold the principles of each era, embracing innovation and creativity. Through the fusion of timing, location, materials, and techniques, they seek to harmonize utility and aesthetics, sensibility and rationality, material and design, occupying both lofty halls and everyday life. Over five thousand years of trials and tribulations, they have converged into a splendid cultural heritage, embodying the ideals, wisdom, magnanimity, and charm of Chinese civilization. Like gentle rain nurturing all things, they instill in the hearts of the Chinese people the spiritual beliefs and lifestyle of benevolence, harmony, and auspiciousness, quietly nurturing the optimism, confidence, and wisdom deep within.
Since the founding of New China, guided by the spirit of "letting a hundred flowers bloom, and a hundred schools of thought contend" and serving the people and socialism, the cause of Arts and Crafts has continuously forged new patterns and shown a new face, playing an important and unique role in the great practice of realizing a better life. In the new era, skilled craftsmen are vividly practicing the concept of "creative transformation and innovative development", shining brightly on a new journey, and will surely contribute to promoting cultural confidence and strength and creating a new splendor of socialist culture.
With majestic rivers and mountains nurturing the beauty of the world, and the fortune of the times flourishing with cultural prosperity, the convergence of craftsmanship creates divine works.
This exhibition, themed "Heavenly Creations", "Masterpieces of a Great Nation", and "A Better Life", showcases the history of Chinese Arts and Crafts, the achievements of Arts and Crafts in New China, and the fascinating chapters of integrating into the new era of a better life.

exhibitions.title: Exhibition of Qinghai Silk Road Artifacts and Qutan Temple Murals
exhibitions.summary:
Due to its historical significance as part of the "Plateau Silk Road", Qinghai has become a geographical symbol of the openness, inclusivity, and multicultural coexistence of Chinese civilization. Qutan Temple is a shining jewel on this symbolic coordinate.
The temple served as an important political link for the central government during the early Ming Dynasty (1368-1644), and its historical significance extends far beyond the religious domain.
The temple's architecture, sculptures, murals, and precious artifacts vividly demonstrate the Sinicization of Tibetan Buddhism on the Qinghai-Tibet Plateau and the harmonious relations between Han and Tibetan cultures. These elements collectively reflect the historical unity, mutual support, and harmonious coexistence among China's diverse ethnic groups over the long course of history.
The "Exhibition of Qinghai Silk Road Artifacts and Qutan Temple Murals" uses murals, artifacts, documents, and multimedia presentations as its framework, weaving a "cultural canal" that transcends time and space.
Here, visitors can see how Han and Tibetan arts have influenced each other in small details, how historical central dynasties integrated Qinghai into the broader national narrative through adaptive governance, the development and transmission of intangible cultural heritage projects, and the systematic protection of cultural heritage. Furthermore, it showcases how modern Qinghai transforms historical legacies into a vibrant source of unity for the Chinese nation.

exhibitions.title: Welcoming Spring: Traditions and New Rhythms in Mianzhu New Year Paintings
exhibitions.summary:
Mianzhu, a renowned cultural hub in Southwest China's Sichuan province, has been a fertile ground for artistic and literary talent since the Qin and Han dynasties (220 BC - 220). This region has long been celebrated for its rich cultural heritage. Notable historical painters and poets have left their mark here, highlighting the area's natural beauty and intellectual legacy. Over the centuries, Mianzhu has become a gathering place for artists and literati, fostering a thriving environment for cultural and artistic endeavors.
The Mianzhu New Year paintings trace their origins back to the Northern Song Dynasty (960-1127) and reached their peak during the Ming and Qing dynasties (1368–1911). These artworks are characterized by their unique blend of hand-painted colors and traditional woodblock printing techniques, involving over 10 intricate processes.
As a treasured form of Chinese folk art, Mianzhu New Year paintings vividly express the enduring wishes of the Chinese people for harmony, prosperity, and good fortune. Each painting encapsulates deep cultural emotions and collective memories, serving as an essential symbol of warmth and familial affection during the Chinese New Year.
This exhibition is organized around five thematic sections, systematically exploring the profound connection between Mianzhu New Year paintings and traditional Chinese New Year customs. Visitors will be guided through the historical evolution, artistic characteristics, and cultural significance of these paintings, gaining insight into their enduring legacy and innovative vitality in the context of modern times.

exhibitions.title: Guo Nian: Photographic Exhibition on the Spring Festival
exhibitions.summary:
The Spring Festival, celebrated as the grandest and most significant traditional festival of the Chinese nation, is akin to an epic masterpiece that marks the beginning of the new year. It paints a vibrant picture of the Chinese civilization's spiritual identity, carrying the profound cultural heritage accumulated over millennia. Serving as a resilient cultural bond, it tightly connects the Chinese people, symbolizing a time for family reunions and shared joy, as well as a vivid manifestation of the Chinese cultural wisdom of "harmony between heaven and humanity" and "coexistence in harmony". As time passes and seasons change, the Spring Festival stands as a marker of the cyclical nature of time and a solemn ritual for bidding farewell to the old and welcoming the new while wishing for a prosperous future. These traditions not only express the ideal of harmony between humanity and nature, but also foster a social atmosphere of kindness, respect, and harmony, inspiring a spirit of striving for excellence.
In December 2024, the Spring Festival, as part of the social practices of the Chinese people in celebration of the traditional new year, was inscribed on the UNESCO Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity. This recognition marks the international community's high regard for the festival.
As a living heritage, the Spring Festival conveys the spiritual essence of the Chinese nation through diverse social practices such as pasting Spring Festival couplets, staying up late on New Year's Eve, and exchanging New Year greetings. It is a cultural practice that involves everyone, reflecting people's lifestyles, values, and social relationships, and plays a crucial role in building a shared spiritual home for the Chinese nation.

exhibitions.title: Intangible Cultural Heritage Exhibition of Hotan Prefecture, Xinjiang
exhibitions.summary:
Hotan, once known as Yutian, is nestled against the majestic Kunlun Mountains to the south and borders the vast Taklamakan Desert to the north. The Hotan, Keriya, and Niya rivers originate from the Kunlun Mountains, and flow northward to create a fertile oasis.
Hotan is renowned for its jade and marks the beginning of the Jade Road. As early as prehistoric times, Hotan jade was transported to the Central Plains. Many jade artifacts unearthed from the Tomb of Fu Hao at the Yin Ruins of the Shang Dynasty (c. 16th century-11th century BC) are made of Hotan jade, a significant component of Chinese jade culture.
Through the ancient Silk Road, advanced agricultural techniques from the Central Plains, such as mulberry planting, silkworm breeding, silk weaving reeling and papermaking, as well as the culture and arts of the Central Plains, were introduced to and flourished in Hotan. Meanwhile, Buddhism, Yutian music and dance, and the Yutian painting school reached the Central Plains. Hotan’s history and culture exemplify the five prominent characteristics of Chinese civilization: continuity, innovation, unity, inclusiveness, and peace. This underscores the historical fact that all ethnic groups in China have jointly developed the vast territory, established a unified multi-ethnic nation, created brilliant history, crafted a splendid culture, and nurtured a great national spirit.
The exhibition is divided into six sections, presenting approximately 30 national and regional intangible cultural heritage projects from Hotan through physical exhibits, video displays, skill demonstrations, and interactive experiences. Nearly 600 items are on display, focusing on projects like the silk dyeing and weaving, construction technique, carpet weaving technique, and mulberry paper making from Xinjiang. During the exhibition, supporting activities such as cultural performances, handicraft experiences, and lectures will offer visitors a comprehensive and immersive understanding of Hotan's intangible cultural heritage and the beauty of Xinjiang.
This exhibition is held to celebrate the 70th anniversary of the founding of the Xinjiang Uygur autonomous region.

exhibitions.title: Splendid Diversity: Practices in Chinese ICH Protection
exhibitions.summary:
The thematic exhibition " Splendid Diversity: Practices in Chinese ICH Protection " opened on June 7, 2024, at the Chinese Traditional Culture Museum.
Guided by the Intangible Cultural Heritage Department of the Ministry of Culture and Tourism and organized by the China Intangible Cultural Heritage Museum, he exhibition features over 100 national intangible cultural heritage projects, including those listed by UNESCO, showcasing more than 600 artifacts across 10 categories of ICH.
The exhibition employs a multi-dimensional approach, using texts, images, real objects, and multimedia interactions to present the contemporary developments in the protection and innovation of ICH.
Emphasizing the dynamic and rich nature of these traditions, the exhibition delves into the cultural significance and modern relevance of intangible cultural heritage, promoting its integration into contemporary life.
The exhibition is divided into three parts:
The first part highlights the protection and inheritance of ICH projects from November 2013 to March 2024, showcasing China's achievements and influence in the field.
The second part features six scenarios. The Traditional Performing Arts section replicates an ancient theater from the Huizhou region, featuring live performances and projected scenes of Peking Opera, Huangmei Opera, Kunqu Opera, and other forms. Sixteen projects are presented, demonstrating the spiritual and cultural depth of Chinese traditional arts.
In the Traditional Fine Arts and Crafts section are 60 projects, including embroidery, New Year paintings, stone carving, wood carving, and construction techniques, emphasizing the craftsmanship and the process from raw materials to finished products.
The Traditional Tea-making Techniques section showcases tea cultivation, production methods, and cultural spaces. Through the lens of tea, it narrates Chinese cultural values and the philosophy of harmony and coexistence.
The Folk Literature and Customs section focuses on 14 projects, including the Three Great Epics and the Dragon Boat Festival, highlighting the role of intangible cultural heritage in connecting ethnic emotions and maintaining national unity.
The third part focuses on China's implementation of the " Convention for the Safeguarding of the Intangible Cultural Heritage " over the past 20 years. It highlights the country's continuous efforts to enhance protection capabilities, expand utilization methods, and establish a unique system for ICH protection.
This part also discusses China's contributions to international heritage protection and its role in promoting intercultural exchange and building a community with a shared future for mankind.
The exhibition will run until June 2025, featuring live demonstrations by 100 national-level inheritors, two heritage-themed streets, interactive experiences in eight experience zones, and fifteen multimedia exhibits. Visitors can immerse themselves in the unique charm of ICH, showcasing the vibrant development and diverse expressions of Chinese intangible cultural heritage.

exhibitions.title: Guo Nian: Bingwu Spring Festival Themed Exhibition
exhibitions.summary:
The Spring Festival is the grandest, most joyous, and harmonious traditional festival for the Chinese people, holding significant importance in the timeline of Chinese life. It serves as a shared spiritual home for the Chinese and a vivid representation and distinctive symbol of the ongoing development of Chinese civilization.
The customs of the Spring Festival are deeply rooted in the simple lives of the people and are born from the cultural creativity of the masses. These customs encompass traditional rituals, agricultural ceremonies, and various folk beliefs that offer spiritual solace and express wishes. They embody the hopes for reunion and harmony, the virtues of respecting the elderly and caring for the young, the principles of friendly neighborly interactions, and the wisdom of living in accordance with time and order. These elements fully and passionately reflect the ancient cultural genes and emotional foundations of the people, forming the enduring vitality and cohesive force of this ancient festival.
In December 2024, the Spring Festival, a social practice of the Chinese people in celebration of the traditional new year, was inscribed on the UNESCO Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity. This year, the CTCM has hosted the "Guo Nian: Bingwu Spring Festival Themed Exhibition" with new ideas, divided into four thematic sections.
The section "Evolution of Time" showcases the evolution of traditional calendar knowledge and New Year celebration customs; "Galloping Steeds" highlights the elegance and symbolic significance of horses in traditional culture through horse-related intangible cultural heritage projects; "Food as Heaven" focuses on the comprehensive view of food, demonstrating the close relationship between intangible cultural heritage protection, Spring Festival customs, and food security through aspects like planting, storage, processing, and sharing; "Lights of Spring" presents the festive atmosphere of family joy with richly shaped and exquisitely crafted lanterns.
May everyone seize the vibrant energy of the season, embodying the elegance and spirit of a gallant horse, and, with dreams as their steed, make the most of their prime!

exhibitions.title: Red Memory Engraved in Thangka: The Innovative Art of Langkajie Thangka
exhibitions.date_range: 2025.12.20 - 2026.01.10 Ying Kiln, Black-sand Ware 2025.12.26 - 2026.01.18 2025 Cross-Strait Calligraphy and Painting Public Exhibition 2025.11.17 - 2025.12.07 Inheritance and Innovation: Anxi Rattan and Ironwork Exhibition 2025.09.27 - 2025.11.03 The 2024 Graduation Exhibition of the Chinese National Academy of Arts Training Center 2025.09.26 - 2025.10.12 The Third National College Arts and Crafts Major Outstanding Graduation Exhibition 2025.09.25 - 2025.10.25
exhibitions.summary:
Luhuo county in Sichuan province has a unique geographical position. This region is home to a diverse population, and through long-term interaction and integration among various ethnic groups, the renowned Langkajie Thangka art, known for its "exquisite and sacred" qualities, has been developed.
In 1936, the Red Army's Long March's arrival here left a profound red imprint. When the rich heritage of intangible cultural heritage meets the great red spirit, an innovative cultural expression emerges, blending tradition with revolutionary history.
The exhibition Red Memory Engraved in Thangka: The Innovative Art of Langkajie Thangka centers on the innovative practice of "red + intangible cultural heritage", vividly interpreting the contemporary theme of strengthening the consciousness of the Chinese national community.
Langkajie Thangka was listed as a national intangible cultural heritage representative project in 2021. Its "thousand-year unfading" mineral pigment characteristic complements the "never-fading" core value of the Long March spirit, providing an eternal artistic medium for recording and passing on collective memory. The various series of works on display not only showcase exquisite craftsmanship but also aim to reconstruct profound historical narratives.
The Langkajie school of painting, with its inclusiveness and innovative vitality, has become a powerful force in telling China's story and building a shared value consensus. This is not only a visual feast but also a profound educational experience that uses beauty as a medium and history as its soul, aiming to connect the past and the future and to inject the vibrant cultural power into the construction of the Chinese national community.

exhibitions.title: The 20th "Arts and Crafts Cup" Beijing Arts and Crafts Competition Achievement Exhibition
exhibitions.summary:
Beijing arts and crafts "sprouted in the State of Yan and Zhao during the Spring and Autumn and Warring States periods (770-221 BC), were established during the Liao and Jin dynasties (916-1234), developed in the Ming and Qing dynasties (1368-1911), and flourished in contemporary times". After a millennium of accumulation, they have formed a distinctive style represented by Beijing's Eight Imperial Handicrafts, or yanjing bajue: gold lacquer inlay, filigree inlaid metal art, cloisonne, jade carving, ivory carving, carved lacquerware, Beijing embroidery, and palace carpets. These exquisite techniques and unique styles stand out in China and are renowned worldwide.
Having been held for 20 editions, the "Arts and Crafts Cup" Beijing Arts and Crafts Competition is not only an annual industry event but also an important initiative by the government to promote the inheritance, innovation, and development of the arts and crafts industry, select outstanding talents, and drive transformation and upgrading.
This exhibition, themed "essence, inheritance, and innovation," systematically showcases the outstanding works from past "Arts and Crafts Cup" competitions, demonstrating the positive achievements of Beijing arts and crafts in contemporary protection and development, and highlighting the practical value of the capital's arts and crafts in serving the people's needs for a better life.

activities.title: CTCM holds 2024 Spring Lantern Festival
activities.summary:
The China National Arts and Crafts Museum (CTCM) · the China Intangible Cultural Heritage Museum will host a lantern-themed event to celebrate the arrival of the Year of the Dragon. The event is being held from February 2nd to March 9th, 2024, and is open to the public from 6:00 PM to 9:00 PM every evening.
This event showcases the distinctive lantern art of Zigong, Sichuan province. Zigong's lantern tradition dates back to the Tang (618-907) and Song (960-1279) dynasties, and involves the lighting of lanterns to usher in Spring Festival and decorating the streets with lanterns during the Lantern Festival. Zigong lanterns mainly include craft lanterns, seat lanterns and group lanterns. Among them, group lanterns are the most distinctive, often depicting themes from outstanding folk traditions, classic literature, and mythological stories. Zigong lanterns use unique materials such as porcelain tableware, glass bottles, silkworm cocoons, thin bamboo strips, tie-dye, silk, and more. Today, Zigong Lantern Festival combines traditional craftsmanship with modern science and technology, creating colorful and dazzling lanterns with a splendid and diverse appearance. In 2008, the Zigong Lantern Festival was included in the second batch of the representative list of China's national intangible cultural heritage.
At this year's event, visitors can enjoy creative lantern themes, such as the Year of the Dragon, the "Classic of Mountains and Rivers", Zigong dinosaurs, and more. They will have the chance to appreciate the unique charm of combining traditional and innovative lantern craftsmanship from Zigong, immerse themselves in the fantastic world of ancient mythical creatures from the "Classic of Mountains and Rivers", and learn related scientific knowledge through the lanterns.
The event covers indoor and outdoor areas, providing visitors with the opportunity to experience and purchase various exquisite handicrafts and intangible cultural heritage creative products, leaving unforgettable memories of their visit. As Spring Festival approaches, let us pray together for the prosperity and stability of the country, and the happiness, well-being, and health of the people.
A lantern of an ancient mythical creature
A lantern of an ancient mythical creature

activities.title: Shanxi Opera Shows Held in Beijing
activities.summary:
The China National Arts and Crafts Museum (CTCM) recently held the "Encounter with Intangible Cultural Heritage" series of social education activities, including two events titled "Elegant Shanxi Opera" and "Opera Grown from Taihang Mountains". These activities provided audiences in Beijing with a one-stop opportunity to appreciate the display of four intangible cultural heritage projects by their inheritors.
Two performers perform Zuoquan-style Xiaohua Opera.
Two performers perform Xiaoyi-style Bowl Tune.
On November 11th, 2023, the China National Arts and Crafts Museum, also known as the China Intangible Cultural Heritage Museum, hosted "Encounter with Intangible Cultural Heritage: Elegant Shanxi Opera - Special Performance from the Jinzhong Cultural Eco-preservation Zone" in conjunction with the exhibition "I Come from Jinzhong - A Thematic Exhibition of Jinzhong Cultural Eco-preservation Zone in the China Intangible Cultural Heritage Museum". On that day, attendees had the opportunity to enjoy three State-level intangible cultural heritage projects: Qitai-style Yangge Dance (a type of Chinese folk dance), Zuoquan-style Xiaohua Opera, and Xiaoyi-style Bowl Tune.
Cui Ejuan (C), a provincial-level inheritor of Shangdang Bangzi, delivers an exceptional performance alongside other performers.
Li Xuebin, an provincial-level inheritor of Shangdang Bangzi, performs.
On November 12th, 2023, the China National Arts and Crafts Museum presented "Encounter with Intangible Cultural Heritage: Opera Grown from Taihang Mountains - A Showcase of Shanxi's Shangdang Bangzi Intangible Cultural Heritage", which mainly showcased Shangdang Bangzi, a school of Chinese opera and State-level intangible cultural heritage in Shanxi. The show allowed visitors to enjoy a performance by State-level and municipal-level inheritors of Shangdang Bangzi from the Shangdang Bangzi Troupe in Changzhi.
The two events were moderated by Gong Yujie, a young cultural scholar from Shanxi. He gave a brief introduction about the origin and development of the four State-level intangible cultural heritage projects, and guided the audience on a journey to experience the unique charm and optimistic attitudes conveyed through the inheritors' wonderful performances.
The shows received wide acclaim, with many people taking photos with performers and expressing great interest in intangible cultural heritage from Shanxi. They added that they were looking forward to visiting the northern province to further appreciate local operas.
